skill.name: debug-cuda-crash
skill.description: Guides developers through debugging FlashInfer CUDA crashes with API logging, tensor metadata, and CUDA diagnostic tools.
skill.summary: Debug FlashInfer CUDA crashes by capturing tensor inputs before failures.
skill.author: flashinfer-ai

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command and weak-crypto patterns, but review shows they are Markdown tutorial examples, environment variables, CUDA debugger commands, and sample output. No prompt injection, exfiltration, credential access, or executable skill code was found; the remaining risk is that users may run diagnostic commands and create logs containing sensitive metadata.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Patterns in Markdown
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography hits occur in prose, sample output, or identifiers inside a Markdown tutorial. No hashing, encryption, signature verification, or cryptographic decision code is present in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is Markdown front matter, environment variable examples, and an options table. I found no executable cryptographic code or security-sensitive crypto usage.

low_findings.title: External Diagnostic Commands Require User Review
low_findings.description: The skill includes shell examples for running Python scripts, torchrun, compute-sanitizer, and cuda-gdb. These are legitimate CUDA debugging steps, but users should review commands before running them in their local environment.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible tutorial examples with placeholder script names and no hidden command construction. They still represent user-executed external commands, so the residual risk is low rather than zero.

low_findings.title: Debug Logs May Expose Local Diagnostic Metadata
low_findings.description: The tutorial demonstrates logs that include CUDA toolkit version, GPU model, PyTorch version, process IDs, and tensor metadata. This is useful for debugging but should be treated as sensitive when logs are saved or shared.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly shows system and tensor metadata being written to logs. This is not malicious, but it creates a clear privacy and operational metadata exposure concern.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Run Shell Debugging Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: Markdown examples instruct users to set environment variables and run Python, torchrun, compute-sanitizer, or cuda-gdb. These commands are expected for CUDA debugging and are not executed by the skill itself.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is confirmed in code blocks, but all examples are transparent and use placeholder local scripts. No command injection or obfuscation was found.

content.value_statement: CUDA crashes often hide the tensor input that caused the failure. This skill shows how to capture FlashInfer API logs before crashes and diagnose shape, dtype, memory, and numerical issues.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to enable FlashInfer API logging with environment variables.
Shows how to inspect logged tensor shapes, dtypes, devices, and statistics.
Maps common CUDA failures to likely input or kernel-level causes.
Demonstrates multi-process log separation for multi-GPU runs.
Combines FlashInfer logs with compute-sanitizer, cuda-gdb, and kernel printf debugging.
limitations: Applies to FlashInfer APIs that use the flashinfer_api logging decorator.
Does not automatically inspect live GPU memory or execute diagnostics.
Requires the user to run commands in a CUDA-capable development environment.
Debug logs may contain tensor metadata and system details that need careful handling.

use_cases.title: Diagnose Illegal Memory Access
use_cases.description: Capture the last FlashInfer API inputs before a CUDA illegal memory access and compare tensor dimensions.

use_cases.title: Investigate NaN or Inf Failures
use_cases.description: Use tensor statistics logging to find numerical overflow, invalid values, or uninitialized data before failure.

use_cases.title: Debug Multi-GPU Training Runs
use_cases.description: Separate logs by process ID and inspect the failing rank without mixing outputs from other workers.

prompt_templates.title: Enable Basic Crash Logging
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a reproducible FlashInfer crash.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me enable FlashInfer API logging for a CUDA crash in my script. Show the minimum environment variables and what output to inspect.

prompt_templates.title: Interpret Tensor Metadata
prompt_templates.scenario: Analyze logs after a crash has been reproduced.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these FlashInfer API log details and identify likely causes. Focus on tensor shape, dtype, device, and contiguity mismatches.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Multi-GPU Debugging
prompt_templates.scenario: Debug a failure that appears only under torchrun or multiple GPU ranks.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a multi-process debugging plan for my FlashInfer CUDA crash. Include per-rank log files and how to isolate the failing rank.

prompt_templates.title: Combine CUDA Tools
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced diagnosis when API logs do not explain a memory error.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a deeper CUDA debugging workflow that combines FlashInfer API logs with compute-sanitizer, cuda-gdb, and kernel printf checks.

output_examples.input: A FlashInfer batch decode call crashes with illegal memory access after enabling level 3 logging.
output_examples.output: The analysis identifies a likely head dimension mismatch between query and key-value cache tensors and recommends matching the final dimension.

output_examples.input: A run fails because outputs contain NaN values after several FlashInfer calls.
output_examples.output: The response recommends statistics logging, checking nan and inf counts, and tracing the first API call where invalid values appear.

output_examples.input: A distributed training job fails on one rank but the combined console output is hard to read.
output_examples.output: The response suggests per-process log files, comparing the failing rank with healthy ranks, and preserving logs after the crash.

best_practices: Start with level 3 logging to capture useful metadata without excessive tensor statistics.
Use level 5 only for numerical issues because statistics can add synchronization and large output.
Disable or lower logging after debugging and treat saved logs as sensitive diagnostic artifacts.
anti_patterns: Do not leave verbose CUDA API logging enabled in production runs.
Do not share debug logs publicly before reviewing tensor metadata and system information.
Do not rely only on console output for crashes because the process may terminate before logs are visible.

faq.question: Does this skill run CUDA commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides instructions and examples for commands the user may run in their own environment.

faq.question: What FlashInfer feature does it use?
faq.answer: It focuses on the flashinfer_api logging decorator and the FLASHINFER_LOGLEVEL and FLASHINFER_LOGDEST environment variables.

faq.question: Can it debug every CUDA crash?
faq.answer: No. It helps identify bad inputs and failure context, but some kernel bugs require lower-level CUDA tooling.

faq.question: When should I use level 5 logging?
faq.answer: Use level 5 when investigating NaN, Inf, or unusual numerical ranges in tensor values.

faq.question: How does it help with multi-GPU runs?
faq.answer: It explains how to write separate logs for each process so a failing rank can be inspected independently.

faq.question: Are debug logs safe to share?
faq.answer: Review them first. They can include tensor metadata, process identifiers, software versions, and system details.
